lessonTitle: Anti-Oppressive/Anti-Racist Practice and Mental Health Resources for Sickle Cell Disease

items.html: Identify elements of trauma informed care
Discuss stressors for people with Sickle Cell Disease, especially upon arrival to the Emergency Department
Identify available mental health resources in Ontario

data.html: To respond to the above, Anti-Oppressive and Anti-Racist approaches integrates the following practices:

images.caption: Connecting the Personal to the Political
Critical Analysis of the system in which patients are embedded

questions.question: Consider your social location and position as a healthcare provider. What are some ways that you see systems of privilege and oppression play out in healthcare on a day-to-day basis?

data.html: An approach that focuses on strengths as opposed to deficits:

items.html: People are resourceful and resilient
Strengths include knowledges, skills, coping strategies, relationships, values, hopes, and dreams
People develop strengths and resilience in the face of adversity
Individuals and families can identify their own strengths. As healthcare workers, we can also support people with sickle cell disease and identify strengths.

data.html: Examples of strengths in people with sickle cell disease, their families, and their communities:

items.html: Faith and Spirituality
Existing coping strategies
Worldview and outlook
Sense of purpose and values
Support networks and community roots
Past experiences and knowledge of solving problems

data.title: Resource
data.html: "Cosplaying to Cope" shows how one person with Sickle Cell Disease draws upon their unique skills and talents to manage their condition and live a fulfilling life. https://www.rbcd.ca/blog/my-sickle-cell-disease-warrior-blood-moon-cosplaying-to-cope

questions.question: Think about a time where you have experienced a personal challenge. How did you get through it? What unique strengths were helpful? What did you learn from that experience?

data.bubble_text: Let's look at what a trauma informed approach to care for patients in the ED, considering what might be running through a patient's mind.

images.caption: Interactive image map illustrating aspects of trauma-informed care in the ED

items.title: Benefits
items.html: Enhance patient engagement • Improve treatment adherence • Promote positive health outcomes • Increase wellness for healthcare workers

items.title: Which Practices Will Build Trust?
items.html: Safety • Trustworthiness & Transparency • Collaboration • Peer Support • Empowerment

items.title: Having Humility
items.html: Humility & Responsiveness • Confidential and Private Space • Creating safer spaces (Being mindful of conversations in public spaces, especially when working with communities facing racism and other forms of discrimination)

data.bubble_text: Now that we have gone over the mental health challenges and strategies for wellness, let's review what Mental Health resources are available to help you help your patient.

items.html:
24/7 Telephone Counselling
  • Suicide Crisis Help Line (Canada-Wide) — Call 988 or visit www.988.ca
  • Kids Help Phone (Canada-wide for youth 29 and under) — Call 1-800-668-6868 or Text 686868
Engage Primary Care
Provider 211 (Finding local community resources) — Call 211 or visit www.211ontario.ca
ConnexOntario (Access to addiction, mental health, and problem gambling services) — Call 1-866-531-2600 or visit www.connexontario.ca
Bounce Back (Coaching support for mental health and wellbeing) — Visit www.bouncebackontario.ca

Ontario Structured Psychotherapy (OHIP-funded psychotherapy from a cognitive behavioural therapy approach) — Visit https://www.ontariohealth.ca/getting-health-care/mental-health-addictions/depression-anxiety-ontario-structured-psychotherapy
Hospital-Based Mental Health Programs
Employment-Based Mental Health Programs and Coverage

questions.question: Write down one thing about how you will commit to practising trauma informed care. This can be a trauma-informed practice you hope to integrate into your work.

data.bubble_text:
Thea Campbell is a person with Sickle Cell Disease who wanted to ensure healthcare professionals incorporate mental health services into the care of their patients, by sharing how she utilizes these essential services.

Note: Thea refers to her disease as "being a carrier to Sickle Cell Anemia", but she does have Sickle Cell Disease and is not a Carrier who would have Sickle Cell Trait.

data.title: Lived Experience Advocate: What Mental Health Resources Do You Use?

items.html: People with sickle cell disease and their families have developed strengths through their experiences of adversity.
Mental Health resources can be a maze to navigate — it's hard to find services. Don't give up and seek help.

questions.question: My knowledge about Mental Health and Wellness in Sickle Cell Disease on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My comfort level with Mental Health and Wellness in SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My confidence level with Mental Health and Wellness for people with SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .

candidates.suggestion: Slide 3 in source is a hybrid image-gallery with a mid-series text panel (#2 of 6 items). Rendered as: opener image (single image_gallery) + text panel (rich_text) + four practice-icon images (gallery image_gallery). No direct LMS equivalent for a mixed image+text slider.
candidates.reason: Source lists 6 items: item 1 = image, item 2 = text panel, items 3–6 = captioned images. Text panel preserved verbatim as rich_text between the images.

candidates.suggestion: Slide 7 (speech-bubble intro) and Slide 8 (image-map) merged onto one slide. Waypoint hotspot content is noted as 'not extractable' in the source; the image is shown with clickForMore/enableLightbox for the learner to explore.
candidates.reason: Image-map waypoint text was not extractable from the source. Rather than emit a near-empty image-only slide, it is attached to the preceding callout as its visual context, satisfying the no-image-only-slides rule.
